key_insight: A split-head Quantum GAN separates lattice bounds from atomic coordinates, and the quantum branch mainly boosts structural diversity while the classical ablation is better at thermodynamic precision.
engineering_relevance: For materials discovery pipelines, this suggests quantum feature mapping can complement physics-informed architectures by reducing mode collapse and widening the search for viable crystalline candidates.
method: Use a split-head generator from a quantum trunk to decouple cell parameters and atomic positions.
Benchmark SH-QGAN against an architecture-matched classical ablation to isolate the effect of quantum circuits.
Test on the constrained Mg-Mn-O chemical system to assess validity, stoichiometry convergence, and diversity.
evidence_signal: On the Mg-Mn-O benchmark, the classical ablation achieved better thermodynamic precision, while SH-QGAN more than doubled geometric validity and generated novel metastable candidates near Mg2MnO4.
watch_item: Results are shown on a highly constrained system, so the gains in diversity may not directly transfer to broader materials spaces or near-term hardware settings.
paper_title: Split-Head Quantum Generative Adversarial Network for Crystalline Material Discovery
